item.title: Shai-Hulud, CVE-2026-20253, AUR Hijacks, and Smishing Kits Drive Threat Coverage
item.description: This digest window is dominated by software supply-chain compromise, identity and trust abuse, and high-impact vulnerabilities in widely deployed platforms. Several reports highlight attackers moving deeper into CI/CD systems, package ecosystems, and authentication infrastructure, while phishing...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by software supply-chain compromise, identity and trust abuse, and high-impact vulnerabilities in widely deployed platforms. Several reports highlight attackers moving deeper into CI/CD systems, package ecosystems, and authentication infrastructure, while phishing operators continue scaling smishing operations with real-time data capture and AI-assisted tooling. Zscaler described continued evolution of the Shai-Hulud campaign, which has expanded from npm into PyPI and shifted from maintainer compromise toward CI/CD abuse. The March 2026 Miasma wave reportedly compromised Aqua Security's Trivy vulnerability scanner via GitHub Actions cache poisoning and used Python .pth files for execution at interpreter startup. The May 2026 Hades wave exploited a pullrequesttarget misconfiguration in the TanStack monorepo, scraped OIDC tokens from runner memory, and published malicious npm packages with valid SLSA provenance attestations. The campaign also introduced an AI-focused evasion technique in a June 8 Hades PyPI wave, embedding prompt injection inside an index.js bundle to influence LLM-based security scanners. ThreatLabz assesses earlier waves are linked to TeamPCP, tracked by Mandiant as UNC6780, but attribution is less certain after public release of the worm source code on May 12, 2026. A separate package ecosystem incident affected the Arch Linux AUR, where attackers hijacked more than 400 packages and modified PKGBUILD or .install scripts to run malicious npm or bun install commands during builds. Reported payloads included a Rust credential stealer distributed through atomic-lockfile@1.4.2 and a later bun-based chain using js-digest, with collection focused on browser data, developer tokens, SSH keys, shell histories, container credentials, VPN profiles, and messaging application session data. The malware also reportedly supported an optional eBPF rootkit when executed with sufficient privileges. watchTowr Labs analyzed CVE-2026-20253, a pre-authentication remote code execution issue in Splunk Enterprise 10 and later involving the PostgreSQL Sidecar Service Endpoint. The researchers reported that Splunk Enterprise on AWS ships with the sidecar installed and enabled by default, and that Splunk's web application could proxy unauthenticated requests to localhost service APIs such as /en-US/splunkd/raw/v1/postgres/recovery/backup. The reported exploit chain abused backup and restore endpoints, path traversal in the backupFile parameter, PostgreSQL connection-string behavior, and Splunk's .pgpass file to obtain controlled arbitrary file write and code execution as the splunk user. Separately, phpBB fixed a decade-old authentication bypass affecting versions 3.3.16 and below and 4.0.0-a2. Aikido reported that the flaw is exploitable in default configurations with a single HTTP request and can allow login as any user, including administrators; phpBB addressed it in version 3.3.17, while no safe 4.x release was available at publication. Censys detailed a USPS-themed smishing campaign that used a fake Cloudflare security check before serving USPS production assets from phishing infrastructure. The kit opened a WebSocket to its origin and streamed victim card data keystroke by keystroke, while performing server-side BIN lookups and returning routing decisions to the browser. Passive DNS pivots expanded one lure hostname into 682 lookalike hostnames across seven confirmed IPs as of a May 20, 2026 snapshot, with related UPS-themed infrastructure identified through banner-hash pivots. Google said it is suing a Chinese cybercrime network accused of operating the Outsider phishing-as-a-service kit and using Gemini AI to generate fraudulent phishing pages supporting SMS phishing attacks against Americans. Google attributed more than 9,000 fake websites and over 1.59 million fraudulent URLs to Outsider between November 14, 2025, and April 14, 2026, and said the service offers more than 290 templates, real-time keystroke logging, and campaign tracking dashboards. A related trust-abuse incident affected Maine's public data breach notification portal, which was temporarily disabled after fraudulent breach disclosures impersonating Discord and VRChat were submitted and automatically published. The Maine Attorney General's Office said the false reports were removed and that it had no knowledge of legitimate recent breach reports from either company. Sygnia reported that the China-nexus group Velvet Ant backdoored PAM and OpenSSH components controlling Linux logins, with earliest traces dating back to 2016. The modified authentication software allowed secret-password access, credential capture, and command logging, enabling persistence even after normal containment steps such as password resets and session termination. The targeted network reportedly lacked direct internet access, so the attackers used internet-facing systems, including a web server, as bridges into the isolated environment. The case underscores how compromise of core login components can convert authentication infrastructure itself into the persistence mechanism, complicating incident scoping and recovery.

item.title: Arch AUR Malware, Coupang Fine, 23andMe Settlement, and Conti Guilty Plea
item.description: This digest is dominated by software supply-chain risk, large-scale data breach fallout, and legal accountability tied to major cyber incidents. The most technically urgent item is the compromise of hundreds of Arch User Repository packages distributing Linux malware, while the largest...
item.content:encoded: This digest is dominated by software supply-chain risk, large-scale data breach fallout, and legal accountability tied to major cyber incidents. The most technically urgent item is the compromise of hundreds of Arch User Repository packages distributing Linux malware, while the largest institutional impacts involve record regulatory penalties, breach settlements, and a ransomware prosecution. More than 400 Arch User Repository packages were reported compromised to distribute a Linux rootkit and infostealer through malicious install scripts. The packages invoked npm to download and execute a malicious package named atomic-lockfile, with at least one sample containing a Linux ELF payload described as a credential stealer with optional root-only eBPF rootkit capabilities. Sonatype reported that the attacker hijacked at least 20 orphaned AUR packages and altered their PKGBUILD files to execute the malicious dependency during installation. A separate report from Flare focused on early warning signs of software supply-chain compromise visible in underground forums and marketplaces. The research highlighted listings for GitHub access, private repositories, source code, API keys, OAuth tokens, cloud credentials, CI/CD data, and package registry access as indicators that may precede public incident disclosure. The article tied these patterns to incidents involving Vercel, Sportradar, Mistral AI, Shai-Hulud, LiteLLM, and malicious VS Code extensions, emphasizing how developer environments and trusted software channels can become attack paths. Together, these reports reinforce a recurring theme: attackers continue to target the trust relationships that underpin modern software delivery. Package maintainers, orphaned projects, developer credentials, CI/CD workflows, and repository access remain high-value assets because compromise at these points can propagate malware or expose downstream organizations. South Korea’s Personal Information Protection Commission imposed a record 624.7 billion won, or approximately $409 million, fine on Coupang and Coupang Fulfillment Services after a breach affecting tens of millions of users. Investigators found that 33,222,472 registered members and at least 4,338,368 non-members were affected, with exposed non-member data including names, phone numbers, and addresses stored as delivery recipient information. The regulator said a former employee used a stolen signing key tied to an alternative authentication system to access data over seven months without detection. The Coupang investigation also included a criminal referral over alleged destruction of evidence after the company manually deleted about six months of web access logs and allowed other records to be automatically wiped. Regulators further found that Coupang covertly collected third-party browsing activity from about 11.2 million users through its Coupang Partners affiliate marketing program without consent. In the United States, a Missouri bankruptcy court administrator approved a $46.8 million settlement fund for victims of the 2023 23andMe data breach. About 7 million customers had data stolen beginning in April 2023, with portions later posted on the dark web. Nearly 256,000 claims have been resolved, with victims eligible for payments ranging from $50 to up to $10,000 depending on claim severity. Ukrainian national Oleksii Oleksiyovych Lytvynenko pleaded guilty in the United States to conspiracy to commit wire fraud for his role in Conti ransomware activity conducted between 2021 and 2022. Prosecutors said he and co-conspirators deployed Conti ransomware against victims in the United States and abroad, stole data, encrypted systems, and extorted ransom payments in Bitcoin. Court documents state that the Conti operation targeted more than 1,000 victims worldwide and collected over $150 million. The case adds to the continuing legal aftermath of the Conti ransomware ecosystem, which became one of the most prominent ransomware operations of its era. Lytvynenko admitted to joining the conspiracy around September 2021, possessing stolen data from eight U.S. victims and four overseas victims, and working on a malware loader for the operation. He was extradited from Ireland after a July 2023 arrest and faces a maximum sentence of 20 years in prison. Separately, Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act was poised to lapse after congressional renewal efforts stalled. A March intelligence court approval may allow existing orders to continue for another year, but the government likely cannot seek new orders if the authority expires. A central unresolved issue is whether major U.S. communications providers will continue complying with Section 702 court orders after expiration. Jamf announced that the JNUC 2026 session catalog is live, with programming centered on the Apple platform for enterprise AI. The agenda includes Mac workflows, mobility workflows, compliance, reporting and risk reduction, integrations and APIs, end-user experience, and community-focused sessions. The event is scheduled for September 23–25, 2026, in Kansas City.

item.title: Agentjacking, CompleteFTP Weak Keys, and Novo Nordisk Clinical Trial Breach
item.description: This digest window was dominated by risks emerging at trusted integration points: AI coding agents consuming external diagnostics, SaaS and CRM workflows carrying attacker-controlled instructions, and enterprise software producing weak cryptographic material. Reporting also highlighted operational...
item.content:encoded: This digest window was dominated by risks emerging at trusted integration points: AI coding agents consuming external diagnostics, SaaS and CRM workflows carrying attacker-controlled instructions, and enterprise software producing weak cryptographic material. Reporting also highlighted operational security concerns around clinical trial data exposure and Windows update deployment failures in managed environments. Tenet Security researchers described “Agentjacking,” a class of attack in which AI coding agents can be induced to execute arbitrary code on developer machines. The reported chain abuses a public Sentry DSN to submit a malicious error event containing crafted markdown; when a developer asks tools such as Claude Code or Cursor to resolve Sentry issues through an MCP server, the agent may interpret attacker-supplied content as trusted diagnostic guidance and run code with the developer’s privileges. The researchers said they identified at least 2,388 organizations with valid injectable DSNs and observed an 85% exploitation success rate in controlled testing across widely used AI coding assistants. The finding reflects a broader theme in this window: AI-enabled workflows are increasingly operating across sensitive trust boundaries, inheriting user permissions, and acting autonomously on data from external systems. That theme also appeared in coverage of AppOmni’s 2026 Intellyx Digital Innovator Award and its SaaS and AI security positioning. The article emphasized that AI-enabled SaaS services expand attack surface because they interact with customer data, inherit permissions, and can operate without posture guardrails; AppOmni highlighted Marlin AI, AskOmni, and AgentGuard as capabilities for SaaS security investigation, GenAI assistance, monitoring, policy management, prompt blocking, and third-party integration control. A separate industry article argued that traditional MDR models struggle with AI-assisted attacker speed and alert volume, citing claims that many alerts go unreviewed and that real threats may originate in low-severity or informational signals. IRONSCALES reported an invoice phishing case in which an attacker used FedEx Freight’s Salesforce CRM to send a rebill request to a logistics and distribution company. The message had no malicious links or attachments, was sent via Salesforce Quick Action, carried a valid DKIM signature for fedexfreight.com, passed DMARC, and returned compauth=100, while a downstream Securence relay introduced an SPF softfail because the relay IP was not in the original sender’s SPF record. The payload was purely textual: a request to change the bill-to entity on freight invoice #0584162950. Themis scored the message at 83% confidence as invoice phishing and four mailboxes were quarantined, illustrating how business email compromise-style activity can leverage legitimate CRM infrastructure and authenticated sender identity rather than conventional malware delivery. Novo Nordisk separately disclosed that attackers accessed internal IT systems and copied non-public clinical trial data. The exposed information included pseudonymized patient IDs, trial participation details, sex, year of birth, biomarkers, health and immunogenicity data, and lifestyle factors such as smoking, alcohol use, and BMI; the company said the data was not directly linked to patient names or other direct identifiers. Information for healthcare professionals was also exposed, including names, registration numbers, email addresses, phone numbers, WhatsApp details, and office locations, while Novo Nordisk said compromised systems were taken offline and core business operations were not impacted. Trail of Bits reported a polynomial-based cryptanalytic technique for factoring RSA moduli with structured, regularly spaced zero-bit patterns. Researchers found hundreds of unique RSA public keys in real-world datasets whose corresponding private keys could be recovered quickly, including keys generated by vulnerable CompleteFTP versions affected by a big-integer type mismatch in which 8-bit random values were copied into 32-bit limbs during key generation. CompleteFTP versions 10.0.0 through 12.0.0 generated vulnerable RSA keys, while versions 10.0.0 through 23.0.4 generated vulnerable DSA keys. The researchers recovered private keys for 603 unique RSA public keys and 74 DSA keys generated by affected versions, and EnterpriseDT released updated detection and checking tools. Microsoft fixed a known issue affecting Windows updates released since May 2025 when installed using the Windows Update Standalone Installer from a network share. The problem affected Windows 11 24H2/25H2 and Windows Server 2025 enterprise systems, producing ERRORBADPATHNAME in scenarios involving WUSA or double-clicked .msu files from network shares containing multiple .msu files; Microsoft addressed it in June 2026 cumulative updates KB5079391 for Windows 11 and KB5094125 for Windows Server 2025.

item.title: Ivanti CVE-2026-10520 Exploitation, LangGraph RCE Chain, and Cybercrime Platform Takedowns
item.description: This digest window is dominated by two parallel themes: high-impact vulnerabilities in exposed enterprise and AI infrastructure, and major law-enforcement disruption of cybercrime service providers. Reporting also highlights a significant public-sector messaging breach affecting French government...
item.content:encoded: This digest window is dominated by two parallel themes: high-impact vulnerabilities in exposed enterprise and AI infrastructure, and major law-enforcement disruption of cybercrime service providers. Reporting also highlights a significant public-sector messaging breach affecting French government users, underscoring continued risk around collaboration platforms and identity compromise. CISA ordered U.S. Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to patch CVE-2026-10520, a maximum-severity OS command injection vulnerability in Ivanti Sentry, within three days under Binding Operational Directive 26-04. The flaw affects Ivanti Sentry, formerly MobileIron Sentry, and was added to CISA’s Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog after confirmed exploitation in attacks. Shadowserver reported that attackers had already backdoored many internet-exposed Ivanti Sentry gateways and warned that unpatched systems are likely compromised. The directive reflects the conditions defined in BOD 26-04: public exposure, KEV listing, potential for automated exploitation at scale, and attacker ability to gain partial or total control of affected systems. Researchers also disclosed three now-patched vulnerabilities in LangGraph, including a critical exploit chain affecting self-hosted deployments. The chain combines CVE-2025-67644, a SQL injection flaw in the SQLite checkpoint implementation affecting langgraph-checkpoint-sqlite versions before 3.0.1, with CVE-2026-28277, an unsafe msgpack deserialization issue affecting langgraph versions before 1.0.10. In self-hosted environments using the SQLite checkpointer with user-controlled filter input, the issues can be chained to execute attacker-controlled code; Check Point said LangChain’s managed LangSmith Deployment platform is not affected by the chain. INTERPOL-led Operation Ramz disrupted the Sniper Dz phishing-as-a-service platform between October 2025 and February 2026. Authorities from 13 Middle East and North Africa countries made 201 arrests, including Guedz, identified as the primary developer and administrator, and seized hardware containing phishing software and scripts. Sniper Dz provided ready-made phishing kits, hosting infrastructure, and operational support targeting brands including PayPal, Facebook, Instagram, Yahoo, Netflix, and Steam. Group-IB said the platform had collected more than 45,000 victim records and was associated with more than 20,000 unique domains, reflecting the industrialized scale of phishing enablement services. Europol also reported the disruption of AudiA6, a cryptocurrency laundering service allegedly used by ransomware gangs and other cybercriminal networks to launder more than €336 million since 2021. The June 10 operation included arrests of two alleged administrators in Georgia, searches, takedowns of 25 domains, seizure of more than 30 servers, and blocking of Telegram accounts used by the network. U.S. authorities charged Ruslan Igorevich Tkachuk and Alexander Vladimirovich Ledenev with money laundering-related offenses, while Europol said AudiA6 relied on thousands of fraudulent exchange accounts and more than 6,000 KYC records tied to money mule accounts. France’s interministerial digital directorate, DINUM, said a breach of the Tchap encrypted messaging platform may have affected 73,467 public-sector employees, representing less than 9% of registered users. The attacker accessed Tchap using a compromised user account, which DINUM said it identified and immediately blocked. DINUM stated that private Tchap conversations remain encrypted and protected, while public chat rooms are not encrypted and their shared data may have been accessed. Potentially exposed account data includes last name, first name, email address, public-sector entity, and avatar. A threat actor claimed responsibility for the breach and alleged they scraped nearly 650,000 messages, information from more than 73,000 accounts, and documents and media files shared through the platform. The incident highlights the exposure created when compromised user accounts intersect with semi-public collaboration spaces, even where private messaging remains encrypted.

item.title: CVE-2026-50751 Check Point VPN Bypass and Cloud DNS Takeover SEO Campaign
item.description: This coverage window is dominated by two concrete exposure patterns: trust boundary failures in internet-facing access infrastructure and abuse of abandoned cloud DNS delegations. The reporting highlights how legacy protocol code and neglected DNS assets can both enable attackers to inherit...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window is dominated by two concrete exposure patterns: trust boundary failures in internet-facing access infrastructure and abuse of abandoned cloud DNS delegations. The reporting highlights how legacy protocol code and neglected DNS assets can both enable attackers to inherit legitimacy from trusted enterprise environments. Check Point released hotfixes on June 8, 2026 for CVE-2026-50751, a critical authentication bypass rated CVSS 9.3 affecting Mobile Access/SSL VPN, Remote Access VPN, and Spark Firewall products. watchTowr Labs analyzed the issue and attributed it to deprecated IKEv1 VPN code, where attacker-controlled input in a VPNExtFeatures Vendor ID payload could influence authentication-related flags inside the gateway implementation. According to the research, the vulnerable implementation could be made to skip certificate signature validation during IKE authentication. watchTowr demonstrated that a vulnerable gateway accepted a random HASHI and created a phase-1 IKE security association, while a patched system rejected the same attempt with an authentication failure. The bypass reportedly worked across UDP 500 and TCP 443 Visitor Mode. The article states that an attacker could authenticate as a provisioned Remote Access user by presenting a matching subject distinguished name, a self-signed X.509 certificate, and an invalid signature, because the gateway did not verify the signature or certificate trust chain. watchTowr reported successful exploitation across Certificate, Certificate with enrollment, and Mixed user authentication modes, making the issue relevant to multiple Remote Access deployment configurations. Cyble Research & Intelligence Labs reported an SEO poisoning campaign that served Thai-language gambling content from trusted enterprise domains by exploiting abandoned or misconfigured DNS infrastructure. The campaign affected 163 organizations across more than 30 countries, with the primary technique involving Azure DNS zone takeover through reclaimed nameserver delegations tied to deleted Azure DNS zones. The campaign also used DigitalOcean DNS zone takeovers, direct wildcard DNS misconfigurations, and individually created A records in at least one Verizon subdomain environment. By inheriting authority over abandoned DNS delegations, the operators were able to publish gambling pages under otherwise legitimate organizational domains, increasing the apparent trust and search visibility of the content. Cyble described a consistent technical footprint across the gambling kit, including valid Let’s Encrypt wildcard TLS certificates, a recurring Next.js build fingerprint, Thai-language SEO content, and affiliate redirect codes such as ibiza99vip1, bigwinv1, link99, and seven77vip1. The infrastructure was linked to three OVH delivery nodes and a 103-node backend fleet in Hong Kong under AS398478, with shared indicators including a broker-xm.com certificate and an identical HTTP body hash.

item.title: Shai-Hulud, AUR Hijacks, Ivanti Sentry, Splunk CVE-2026-20253, and AI Agent RCE
item.description: This 24-hour digest is dominated by software supply-chain compromise, authentication and remote-access vulnerabilities, and phishing ecosystems increasingly abusing trusted platforms and AI-enabled workflows. Reporting also highlights long-running intrusion tradecraft, large-scale data exposure...
item.content:encoded:
This 24-hour digest is dominated by software supply-chain compromise, authentication and remote-access vulnerabilities, and phishing ecosystems increasingly abusing trusted platforms and AI-enabled workflows. Reporting also highlights long-running intrusion tradecraft, large-scale data exposure consequences, and law-enforcement disruption of cybercrime infrastructure. The Shai-Hulud campaign continued to evolve beyond its npm origins into PyPI and CI/CD environments, with Zscaler describing Miasma and Hades waves that abused GitHub Actions cache poisoning, Python .pth persistence, pullrequesttarget misconfiguration, OIDC token scraping, and malicious packages published with valid SLSA provenance. The June 8 Hades PyPI wave also embedded prompt injection intended to mislead LLM-based security scanners, while the public release of the worm source code under an MIT license has made later attribution less certain. Arch Linux’s AUR saw a major package compromise affecting more than 400 packages, where modified build scripts pulled malicious npm or Bun-delivered payloads such as atomic-lockfile and js-digest. The malware targeted developer credentials, browser and Electron app sessions, GitHub and npm tokens, Vault tokens, SSH keys, container credentials, shell histories, and VPN profiles, with optional eBPF rootkit functionality when executed with sufficient privileges. Broader supply-chain risk reporting focused on dark web precursors, including advertisements for GitHub access, private repositories, source code, API keys, CI/CD data, cloud credentials, and OAuth tokens. Separately, Trail of Bits and the badkeys project identified hundreds of factorable “short-sleeve” RSA and DSA keys tied in part to flawed key generation in older EnterpriseDT CompleteFTP versions. watchTowr detailed CVE-2026-20253, a pre-authentication remote code execution path in Splunk Enterprise 10+ involving the PostgreSQL Sidecar Service. Although the service listens on localhost, researchers showed it could be reached through Splunk web routes and chained backup and restore behavior with PostgreSQL features to achieve controlled file write as the splunk user and overwrite a Splunk Python script. CISA ordered rapid federal remediation of Ivanti Sentry CVE-2026-10520, a maximum-severity OS command injection flaw under active exploitation, with Shadowserver reporting widespread exploitation and many already-backdoored exposed gateways. Check Point also released hotfixes for CVE-2026-50751, an IKEv1 authentication bypass in Mobile Access, Remote Access VPN, and Spark Firewall products, where crafted VPN extension data could cause certificate signature validation to be skipped. Other exposed software included phpBB, which fixed a decade-old authentication bypass allowing login as any user in default configurations, and LangGraph, where a SQL injection flaw and unsafe msgpack deserialization could be chained to remote code execution in certain self-hosted deployments. Microsoft also resolved Windows update installation failures affecting WUSA use from network shares on Windows 11 24H2/25H2 and Windows Server 2025. Tenet Security described “Agentjacking,” an attack class in which malicious Sentry error events containing crafted markdown can be retrieved through an MCP server and interpreted by AI coding agents such as Claude Code or Cursor as trusted diagnostic guidance. The attack begins with a public Sentry DSN and can lead to code execution on developer systems when agents act on attacker-controlled issue content. AI and SaaS security themes also appeared in reporting on AppOmni’s award announcement and commentary on MDR evolution, both emphasizing expanding attack surfaces as AI-enabled services inherit permissions, interact with enterprise data, and automate activity. These pieces framed AI as both an operational security dependency and a potential accelerator for attacker and defender workflows. Censys traced a USPS-themed smishing kit from a single hostname to 682 lookalike domains, with fake Cloudflare verification, USPS production assets, live Google Analytics reuse, WebSocket-based keystroke streaming of payment data, server-side BIN lookups, and operator-controlled routing prompts. Google separately sued a Chinese cybercrime network accused of operating the Outsider phishing-as-a-service kit and using Gemini AI to support smishing, with Google attributing 9,000 fake sites and more than 1.59 million fraudulent URLs to the service. INTERPOL’s Operation Ramz disrupted the Sniper Dz phishing-as-a-service platform, with 201 arrests across 13 countries and seizure of infrastructure tied to more than 45,000 victim records and over 20,000 domains. In another trust-abuse case, an attacker used FedEx Freight’s Salesforce CRM to send a DKIM-valid, DMARC-passing invoice rebill request with no link or attachment payload. Cyble reported a large SEO poisoning campaign exploiting abandoned cloud DNS delegations, especially Azure DNS zone takeovers, to host Thai-language gambling content under the domain authority of 163 organizations. The campaign used wildcard records, valid Let’s Encrypt certificates, affiliate redirect codes, and geo-filtering to route Thai users to gambling destinations. Sygnia attributed a long-running intrusion to China-linked Velvet Ant, which backdoored Linux PAM and OpenSSH login components to enable secret-password access, credential and command logging, and log-disabling controls. The activity reportedly dated back to 2016 and persisted in a network segment without direct internet access by bridging through internet-facing systems. Several breach developments centered on sensitive personal and organizational data. Novo Nordisk disclosed theft of pseudonymized clinical trial data and healthcare professional contact details, France reported that 73,467 Tchap messenger accounts were affected after access through a compromised account, and South Korea fined Coupang $409 million after a breach affecting tens of millions of customers and recipients. Maine temporarily disabled public access to its breach notification portal after fraudulent disclosures impersonating Discord and VRChat were automatically published. A bankruptcy administrator also approved a $46.8 million settlement fund for victims of the 2023 23andMe breach, while a Ukrainian national pleaded guilty for his role in Conti ransomware operations and Europol disrupted the AudiA6 crypto-laundering service allegedly used to clean more than €336 million since 2021.

Marking Your Own Homework (Check Point Remote Access VPN IKEv1 Authentication Bypass CVE-2026-50751) — Must Read

watchTowr Labs found that CVE-2026-50751 in Check Point Remote Access VPN lets an attacker use client-controlled IKEv1 Vendor ID flags to skip certificate and signature validation, allowing login as a valid user with a self-signed certificate and random signature. The flaw affects legacy IKEv1 remote access paths, including UDP 500 and TCP 443 Visitor Mode, and is fixed by Check Point hotfix sk185033.

  • Check Point released hotfixes on June 8, 2026 for CVE-2026-50751, a 9.3 CVSS authentication bypass affecting Mobile Access/SSL VPN, Remote Access VPN, and Spark Firewall products in deprecated IKEv1 VPN code.
  • watchTowr Labs found that the vulnerable IKEv1 implementation allowed a client-supplied VPNExtFeatures Vendor ID payload to write authentication flags that could cause certificate signature checks to be skipped.
  • The researchers demonstrated that a vulnerable gateway accepted a random HASH_I and created a phase-1 IKE SA, while a patched gateway rejected the same attempt with an authentication failure.
  • The article states that an attacker could authenticate as a provisioned Remote Access user by presenting a matching subject distinguished name, a self-signed X.509 certificate, and an invalid signature, because the gateway did not verify the signature or certificate trust chain.
  • watchTowr Labs reported that the bypass worked over both UDP 500 and TCP 443 Visitor Mode, and across the Certificate, Certificate with enrollment, and Mixed user authentication modes.

CISA orders feds to patch actively exploited Ivanti flaw by Sunday — Must Read

CISA ordered federal agencies to patch actively exploited Ivanti Sentry flaw CVE-2026-10520 within three days under BOD 26-04. Shadowserver warns that unpatched internet-exposed Ivanti Sentry systems are likely already compromised after widespread exploitation using a public proof-of-concept.

  • CISA ordered Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies to patch the actively exploited Ivanti Sentry vulnerability CVE-2026-10520 within three days under Binding Operational Directive 26-04.
  • CVE-2026-10520 is a maximum-severity OS command injection vulnerability in Ivanti Sentry, formerly known as MobileIron Sentry.
  • Shadowserver reported that attackers had already backdoored many internet-exposed Ivanti Sentry gateways and observed widespread exploitation attempts based on a public proof-of-concept.
  • Shadowserver warned that organizations that had not patched their Ivanti Sentry instances were most likely compromised, although its detection was limited because some instances were not reachable in its scans.
  • CISA added CVE-2026-10520 to its Known Exploited Vulnerabilities Catalog and said this type of vulnerability poses significant risks to the federal enterprise.

Shai-Hulud Campaign Evolution: Miasma, Hades, and AI Scanner Evasion — Must Read

Shai-Hulud has evolved from npm maintainer compromise into a broader supply-chain threat abusing PyPI, GitHub Actions OIDC publishing, IDE configuration files, and LLM scanner prompt injection. The key takeaway is that valid provenance and automated AI-based scanning are not enough if CI/CD systems, developer tools, and package analysis pipelines can be manipulated.

  • The Shai-Hulud campaign expanded beyond npm into PyPI, shifted from maintainer-focused compromise to CI/CD abuse, and introduced execution through IDE configuration files and prompt injection against AI-based security scanners.
  • In March 2026, the Miasma wave compromised Aqua Security's Trivy scanner through GitHub Actions cache poisoning and used Python .pth files for persistence in site-packages.
  • In May 2026, the Hades/TanStack wave abused a pull_request_target misconfiguration to scrape OIDC tokens from GitHub Actions runner memory and publish malicious packages with valid SLSA provenance.
  • On May 12, 2026, the complete worm source code was publicly released under an MIT license, making later attribution less certain because the toolkit became publicly reusable.
  • The June 8, 2026 Hades PyPI wave embedded prompt injection in an _index.js bundle to mislead LLM-based security scanners into producing false-clean verdicts or no usable verdict.

Why Use App-Level Auth When Every Database Has Auth? (Splunk Enterprise CVE-2026-20253 Pre-Auth RCE) — Must Read

Splunk Enterprise CVE-2026-20253 is a pre-auth flaw in the PostgreSQL Sidecar Service that can be reached through Splunk’s web interface and abused from arbitrary file creation into full remote code execution. Splunk Enterprise on AWS is reportedly vulnerable by default, making patching or mitigation urgent.

  • Splunk’s advisory for CVE-2026-20253 describes an unauthenticated vulnerability in the PostgreSQL Sidecar Service endpoint affecting Splunk Enterprise versions 10 and above.
  • The article states that Splunk Enterprise on AWS has the PostgreSQL Sidecar Service installed and enabled by default, while manually installed on-premise deployments differ in whether the service is installed or enabled by default.
  • The researchers found that the PostgreSQL Sidecar Service listens on localhost but can be reached through the main Splunk web application via the /en-US/splunkd/__raw/v1/postgres/recovery/backup and /restore paths.
  • The backup endpoint allows arbitrary file creation and truncation through the backupFile parameter, and the database parameter can be abused as a PostgreSQL connection string to make Splunk connect to an attacker-controlled database.
  • By combining the backup and restore endpoints with PostgreSQL features including passfile and lo_export, the researchers demonstrated a controlled arbitrary file write as the splunk user and then achieved remote code execution by overwriting a Splunk Python script.

Over 400 Arch Linux AUR Packages Hijacked to Deploy Infostealer and eBPF Rootkit — Must Read

Attackers hijacked hundreds of Arch Linux AUR packages by altering build scripts to run malicious npm/bun dependencies that install a Rust credential stealer and, when run as root, an optional eBPF rootkit. Anyone who built or updated affected AUR packages since June 11 should treat the host and developer credentials as compromised, with rebuild/reinstall advised if the payload ran as root.

  • Attackers took over more than 400 Arch User Repository packages and changed their build scripts to install a credential-stealing Rust binary on machines that built them.
  • The malicious build changes used npm install atomic-lockfile and, in a second wave, bun install js-digest to pull payloads during the AUR build process.
  • The stealer targets developer secrets including browser data, Electron app sessions, GitHub and npm tokens, Vault tokens, SSH keys, shell histories, Docker and Podman credentials, and VPN profiles.
  • When run with root privileges and the required capability, the malware can load an optional eBPF rootkit that hides its processes, names, and socket inodes from standard tools.
  • The article advises users who installed or updated AUR packages on or after June 11 to check affected-package lists, hunt for persistence, rotate exposed credentials, and reinstall from trusted media if the payload ran as root.

South Korea hits Coupang with record $409 million fine over data breach — Must Read

South Korea’s privacy regulator fined Coupang a record $409 million after finding that basic security failures allowed a former employee to steal data on more than 33 million customers and millions of non-members. Regulators also accused Coupang of failing to notify victims, deleting evidence, and committing additional privacy violations.

  • South Korea’s Personal Information Protection Commission fined Coupang and Coupang Fulfillment Services 624.7 billion won, or $409 million, after finding that a data breach resulted from deficiencies in basic safety management.
  • The investigation confirmed that 33,222,472 registered Coupang members were affected and identified at least 4,338,368 non-members whose names, phone numbers, and addresses had been stored as delivery recipients.
  • The regulator said a former Coupang employee used a stolen signing key for an alternative authentication system to access customer data over a seven-month period without being detected by the company.
  • The commission referred Coupang for criminal prosecution over destruction of evidence after the company manually deleted about six months of web access logs and allowed additional records to be automatically wiped.
  • The investigation also found that Coupang covertly collected third-party browsing activity from about 11.2 million users through its Coupang Partners affiliate marketing program without consent.

China-Linked Hackers Backdoored Linux Login Software to Hide for Nearly a Decade — Must Read

China-linked Velvet Ant hid for nearly a decade by backdooring Linux PAM and OpenSSH login components, letting them steal credentials and retain access even after normal cleanup. The key takeaway is that defenders must verify the integrity of trusted infrastructure like login software, not just reset passwords or patch known CVEs.

  • Sygnia says the China-nexus group Velvet Ant backdoored Linux PAM and OpenSSH components that control user logins.
  • The earliest traces of the activity go back to 2016, and the attacker modified trusted login programs instead of dropping new malware.
  • The backdoored PAM and OpenSSH components enabled secret-password access, credential logging, command logging, and a hidden switch to disable logging when needed.
  • The targeted network had no direct internet access, so the attacker used internet-facing systems, including a web server, as a bridge into the isolated segment.
  • The article says normal containment measures such as password resets and killed sessions were ineffective while the compromised login system remained in place.

phpBB forum fixes auth bypass bug lurking for a decade — Should Read

phpBB fixed a decade-old authentication bypass in version 3.3.17 that could let attackers log in as any user, including administrators, with a single HTTP request. Forum operators on phpBB 3.3.16 or earlier should upgrade immediately, while 4.x users do not yet have a safe release.

  • A 10-year-old authentication bypass vulnerability in phpBB allows an attacker to log in as any user, including administrators, with a single HTTP request.
  • The flaw impacts phpBB versions 3.3.16 and below and 4.0.0-a2, with phpBB fixing the issue in version 3.3.17 for the 3.x branch.
  • Aikido discovered the bug on June 2 and reported it through phpBB’s HackerOne Vulnerability Disclosure Program, and phpBB addressed it on June 6.
  • Aikido said the vulnerability is exploitable in the default configuration and requires no special knowledge.
  • Aikido withheld technical details to give forum administrators time to update and said full details will be published in a future report.

Borrowed Trust – Systematic Exploitation of Abandoned Cloud DNS Delegations to serve Thai Gambling SEO Content — Should Read

Cyble found a large SEO poisoning campaign abusing abandoned cloud DNS delegations, mainly Azure DNS zones, to host Thai gambling pages under trusted enterprise subdomains. The key takeaway is that stale NS delegations can let attackers reclaim orphaned zones, obtain valid TLS certificates, and silently exploit an organization’s domain reputation without breaching its systems.

  • Cyble Research & Intelligence Labs identified an active SEO poisoning campaign that exploited abandoned cloud DNS zone delegations to serve Thai-language gambling content under the domain authority of 163 organizations across more than 30 countries.
  • The primary mechanism was Azure DNS zone takeover, where the actor claimed orphaned Azure DNS zones still delegated from victim parent domains, added wildcard A records, and obtained valid Let's Encrypt wildcard TLS certificates.
  • The campaign also used DigitalOcean DNS zone takeovers, direct wildcard DNS misconfigurations, and individually created A records, including more than 1,000 gambling-keyword subdomains under cardsforheroes.verizon.com.
  • Cyble linked the campaign to three OVH delivery IPs and a separate 103-node backend fleet in Hong Kong within AS398478, with shared fingerprints including a broker-xm.com certificate, a common JARM fingerprint, and an identical HTTP body MD5 hash.
  • The gambling kit used Thai-language Next.js pages, affiliate redirect codes such as ibiza99vip1, bigwinv1, link99, and seven77vip1, and server-side geographic filtering that verified Thai referrers before redirecting users.

The Package That Never Shipped: Following a USPS Smishing Kit Through Censys DNS Data — Should Read

A USPS-themed smishing link led researchers to a larger phishing operation with hundreds of disposable domains, real-time card skimming, and a related UPS campaign hosted on Tencent Cloud. The most durable detection points are not the rotating domains or IPs but structural artifacts like asset paths, cookies, token formats, banner hashes, and Censys DNS history.

  • A USPS-themed smishing campaign used a link at usps.xupqnqz[.]one that resolved to Tencent Cloud IP 43.157.174.200 and displayed a fake Cloudflare verification page before loading the phishing content.
  • Censys passive DNS data expanded the investigation from one hostname to a cluster with 682 unique lookalike hostnames as of the 2026-05-20 snapshot, many of which no longer resolved in live DNS.
  • The USPS phishing kit served USPS production HTML, CSS, fonts, and images under the /us_post_usps/ path and included USPS’s live Google Analytics 4 property G-CSLL4ZEK4L.
  • The kit opened a WebSocket to its origin and streamed victim payment-card input keystroke by keystroke, performed a server-side BIN lookup, and could push routing decisions such as retry, PIN prompt, OTP prompt, or kill-switch back to the victim’s browser.
  • Pivoting on an HTTP banner hash identified five related UPS-themed hosts using a Spring Boot backend, and those hosts exposed cookies containing the internal theme name us_post_ups.

Over 73,000 French govt employees affected in Tchap messenger breach — Should Read

France’s DINUM says a breach of the Tchap government messaging platform exposed data from 73,467 public sector employees, including names, emails, organizations, and avatars from unencrypted public rooms. Private conversations remained encrypted, but the attacker claims to have scraped hundreds of thousands of messages and stolen shared files and metadata.

  • The French government said a breach of its Tchap encrypted messaging platform affected 73,467 public sector employees, representing less than 9% of registered users.
  • DINUM said the attacker used a compromised user account to access Tchap and that the account behind the malicious requests was identified and blocked.
  • DINUM said private conversations on Tchap remain encrypted, but public chat rooms are not encrypted and may have exposed users’ names, email addresses, avatar images, and public sector organizations.
  • A threat actor claimed responsibility for the breach and said they gained access through a social engineering attack.
  • The threat actor claimed to have scraped nearly 650,000 messages and information from more than 73,000 accounts, as well as over 13.5GB of documents and media files shared on Tchap.

Google Sues Chinese Smishing Network Accused of Using Gemini AI in Phishing — Should Read

Google is suing a China-based smishing network accused of using its Gemini AI to help create phishing pages and run large-scale text-message scams targeting Americans. The Outsider phishing-as-a-service kit allegedly enabled thousands of fake sites, millions of fraudulent URLs, and losses affecting more than 100,000 victims.

  • Google is pursuing legal action against a Chinese cybercrime network accused of using its Gemini AI agent to send phishing text messages targeting Americans.
  • The network allegedly developed and managed the Outsider phishing-as-a-service kit, which enables criminals to create fraudulent websites, launch phishing campaigns, and steal credit card numbers, bank credentials, and personal data.
  • Google said Outsider operations are coordinated through Telegram and have victimized more than 100,000 people, causing millions of dollars in losses.
  • Between November 14, 2025, and April 14, 2026, Google identified 9,000 fake websites and more than 1.59 million fraudulent URLs tied to the phishing service.
  • Google said it is partnering with AT&T, T-Mobile, and Verizon to block Outsider-related smishing messages from reaching customers.

Agentjacking Attack Tricks AI Coding Agents Into Running Malicious Code — Should Read

Researchers described “Agentjacking,” a technique that abuses fake Sentry error reports to make AI coding agents like Claude Code and Cursor treat attacker-supplied instructions as trusted fixes and run malicious code on developers’ machines. The issue stems from AI agents trusting external MCP-fed data, potentially exposing credentials and other sensitive developer information without compromising the target’s infrastructure.

  • Tenet Security researchers described Agentjacking as a new class of attack that can trick AI coding agents into running arbitrary code on developer machines.
  • The attack uses a fake Sentry error report containing crafted markdown that is returned through the Sentry MCP server and interpreted by AI agents as trusted diagnostic guidance.
  • The attack chain begins with an attacker finding a target’s public Sentry DSN and sending a malicious error event to Sentry’s ingest endpoint.
  • When a developer asks an AI coding agent such as Claude Code or Cursor to fix unresolved Sentry issues, the agent can query Sentry via MCP and execute the attacker-controlled code with the developer’s privileges.
  • Tenet Security said it found at least 2,388 organizations exposed with valid injectable DSNs and achieved an 85% exploitation success rate in controlled testing against injected errors across widely used AI coding assistants.

INTERPOL Operation Takes Down Sniper Dz Phishing Platform, Arrests Administrator — Should Read

INTERPOL’s Operation Ramz dismantled the Sniper Dz phishing-as-a-service platform and led to 201 arrests across the MENA region, including its alleged administrator in Algeria. The decade-old service enabled large-scale phishing with ready-made kits and infrastructure tied to more than 20,000 domains and 45,000 victim records.

  • INTERPOL-led Operation Ramz disrupted the Sniper Dz phishing-as-a-service platform between October 2025 and February 2026.
  • Authorities from 13 countries in the Middle East and North Africa region made 201 arrests, including Guedz, the primary developer and administrator of Sniper Dz.
  • Authorities took down the website used to offer Sniper Dz’s phishing-as-a-service capabilities and seized hardware containing phishing software and scripts.
  • Group-IB said Sniper Dz had collected more than 45,000 victim records and was associated with more than 20,000 unique domains.
  • Sniper Dz offered ready-made phishing kits, hosting infrastructure, and operational support, targeting major organizations such as PayPal, Facebook, Instagram, Yahoo, Netflix, and Steam.

Major US surveillance program poised to lapse after legislative deadlock — Should Read

Section 702 of FISA, a major U.S. foreign surveillance authority used for national security intelligence, is set to lapse after Congress failed to pass an extension amid political deadlock over intelligence leadership and reform demands. Existing surveillance orders may continue for now, but new orders and telecom provider compliance could face legal uncertainty.

  • Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act is set to expire at midnight for the first time since it became law in 2008.
  • Congressional efforts to renew Section 702 stalled after President Donald Trump announced Bill Pulte as acting director of national intelligence, angering Democrats and surprising Republicans.
  • The House and Senate both failed to advance short-term or fast-tracked renewals of Section 702 before the deadline.
  • A March intelligence court approval may allow existing Section 702 orders to continue for another year, but the government likely cannot seek new orders if the law lapses.
  • A key unresolved issue is whether major U.S. communications providers will continue complying with Section 702 court orders if the program expires.

Factoring "short-sleeve" RSA keys with polynomials — Should Read

Researchers found hundreds of real-world RSA and DSA keys with structured zero-bit patterns that made them recoverable, mostly traced to a CompleteFTP big-integer random generation bug present in older versions. Users who generated CompleteFTP host keys during the affected periods should check and regenerate vulnerable keys.

  • Researchers working with Hanno Böck of the badkeys project found hundreds of RSA public keys with heavily zero-biased, structured bit patterns that could be quickly factored.
  • The researchers traced one short-sleeve key pattern to a type mismatch in big-integer code in old versions of EnterpriseDT CompleteFTP, where 8-bit RNG output was copied into 32-bit big-integer limbs.
  • The CompleteFTP issue affected RSA keys generated with versions 10.0.0 through 12.0.0 from December 2016 to March 2019 and DSA keys generated with versions 10.0.0 through 23.0.4 from December 2016 to December 2023.
  • The researchers recovered private keys for 603 unique RSA public keys and 74 DSA keys generated by vulnerable CompleteFTP versions, plus 26 RSA keys with an unidentified short-sleeve pattern.
  • EnterpriseDT released CompleteFTP v26.1.0 and a standalone KeyChecker tool to detect vulnerable RSA or DSA keys and alert users when regeneration is needed.

The FedEx Freight Invoice That Came From Inside the CRM — Should Read

An attacker used FedEx Freight’s legitimate Salesforce CRM to send an authenticated invoice rebill request that passed DKIM and DMARC, with no malicious links or attachments. The key risk was business email compromise via believable invoice-change text from trusted infrastructure, requiring verification through known contacts rather than email authentication alone.

  • An attacker used FedEx Freight's Salesforce CRM to send an invoice rebill request to a logistics and distribution company.
  • The email was dispatched via Salesforce Quick Action, producing a valid DKIM signature for fedexfreight.com and passing DMARC with compauth=100.
  • The message contained no malicious links or attachments, and the payload was a text request to change the bill-to entity on FedEx Freight invoice #0584162950.
  • The email was relayed through a Securence gateway, which caused an SPF softfail because the relay IP was not in the original sender's SPF record.
  • Themis scored the message at 83% confidence as invoice phishing and quarantined four mailboxes.

Pharma giant Novo Nordisk discloses breach of clinical trials data — Should Read

Novo Nordisk disclosed that attackers accessed internal systems and copied pseudonymized clinical trial patient data, as well as contact details for some healthcare professionals. The company says patients cannot be directly identified from the exposed trial data, but affected healthcare professionals may face phishing attempts.

  • Novo Nordisk disclosed that attackers accessed internal IT systems and copied non-public data related to patients participating in some clinical trials.
  • The exposed clinical trial data included patient IDs, trial participation information, sex, year of birth, biomarkers, health and immunogenicity data, and lifestyle factors such as smoking, alcohol use, and BMI.
  • Novo Nordisk said the clinical trial data was pseudonymized and not directly linked to patients by name or other direct identifiers.
  • The breach also exposed data for an undisclosed number of healthcare professionals, including names, registration numbers, email addresses, phone numbers, WhatsApp details, and office locations.
  • Novo Nordisk took the compromised internal IT systems offline and said its core business operations were not impacted.

LangGraph Flaw Chain Exposes Self-Hosted AI Agents to Remote Code Execution — Should Read

Researchers disclosed three patched LangGraph flaws, including a chain in self-hosted deployments that can combine SQL injection with unsafe deserialization to achieve remote code execution. Users running affected SQLite or Redis checkpointers should update immediately and restrict exposure of LangGraph servers and checkpoint access.

  • Cybersecurity researchers disclosed three now-patched vulnerabilities affecting LangGraph, including a critical chain that could lead to remote code execution.
  • CVE-2025-67644 is a SQL injection flaw in LangGraph's SQLite checkpoint implementation that affects langgraph-checkpoint-sqlite versions before 3.0.1.
  • CVE-2026-28277 is an unsafe msgpack deserialization flaw in LangGraph that affects langgraph versions before 1.0.10.
  • CVE-2025-67644 and CVE-2026-28277 can be chained in self-hosted deployments using the SQLite checkpointer with user-controlled filter input to execute attacker-controlled code.
  • Check Point said LangChain's managed LangSmith Deployment platform is not affected by the vulnerability chain.

Europol Disrupts AudiA6 Crypto Laundering Service Used by Ransomware Gangs — Should Read

Europol and international partners disrupted AudiA6, a crypto laundering service allegedly used by ransomware gangs to wash more than €336 million since 2021. Two alleged administrators were arrested in Georgia, infrastructure was seized, and U.S. charges could carry up to 20 years in prison.

  • Europol said authorities disrupted AudiA6, a cryptocurrency laundering service used by ransomware gangs and cybercriminal networks to launder more than €336 million since 2021.
  • The June 10, 2026 operation included the arrest of two alleged administrators in Georgia, searches, takedowns of 25 domains, seizure of more than 30 servers, and blocking of Telegram accounts used by the network.
  • The U.S. Department of Justice charged Ruslan Igorevich Tkachuk and Alexander Vladimirovich Ledenev with conspiracy to launder monetary instruments and sting money laundering, each carrying a maximum possible sentence of 20 years in prison if convicted.
  • Europol said AudiA6 used thousands of fraudulent exchange accounts opened with stolen or purchased identities and identified more than 6,000 KYC records linked to money mule accounts.
  • AudiA6 allegedly operated as a cryptocurrency mixing service that returned “cleaned” funds within an hour through complex transaction chains while charging commissions between 3 percent and 10 percent.

Ukrainian national pleads guilty to role in Conti ransomware operation — Should Read

Ukrainian national Oleksii Lytvynenko pleaded guilty in the U.S. to helping the Conti ransomware operation by developing malware tooling and possessing stolen victim data. He faces up to 20 years in prison for his role in attacks tied to a group that extorted over $150 million from more than 1,000 victims worldwide.

  • Ukrainian national Oleksii Oleksiyovych Lytvynenko pleaded guilty in the United States to conspiracy to commit wire fraud for his role in Conti ransomware attacks conducted between 2021 and 2022.
  • Prosecutors said Lytvynenko and co-conspirators deployed Conti ransomware on victim networks in the United States and abroad, stealing data and encrypting devices to extort Bitcoin ransom payments.
  • Lytvynenko admitted to joining the Conti conspiracy around September 2021, possessing data stolen from eight U.S. victims and four overseas victims, and working on a malware loader for the operation.
  • Court documents state that the Conti ransomware operation targeted more than 1,000 victims worldwide and collected over $150 million in ransom payments.
  • Lytvynenko was extradited from Ireland to the United States after his July 2023 arrest and now faces a maximum sentence of 20 years in prison.

Microsoft fixes Windows update failures linked to WUSA installer — May Read

Microsoft fixed a Windows 11 24H2/25H2 and Windows Server 2025 issue that caused updates to fail when installed with WUSA from network shares containing multiple .msu files. The fix is included in the June 2026 cumulative updates, with a workaround of copying .msu files locally for older updates.

  • Microsoft fixed a known issue that caused Windows updates released since May 2025 to fail when installed via the Windows Update Standalone Installer from a network share.
  • The issue affects Windows 11 24H2/25H2 and Windows Server 2025 devices on enterprise networks, and it does not occur with a single .msu file or when the files are stored locally.
  • Microsoft said the failures could occur with ERROR_BAD_PATHNAME when WUSA or double-clicking a .msu file is used from a network share containing multiple .msu files.
  • Microsoft addressed the issue for all affected systems in the June 2026 cumulative updates KB5079391 for Windows 11 and KB5094125 for Windows Server 2025.
  • Microsoft advised users of older updates to work around the issue by saving the .msu files locally on the device and installing the update from that location.

Maine disables data breach notification portal after fake disclosures — May Read

Maine temporarily took its public data breach notification portal offline after fake disclosures impersonating VRChat and Discord were automatically published. The state removed the hoaxes and is reviewing procedures to prevent abuse of the reporting system.

  • Maine temporarily disabled public access to its data breach notification database after fraudulent breach disclosures were submitted and published on the state's website.
  • The fake disclosures impersonated Discord and VRChat, and VRChat confirmed that its filing was fraudulent and submitted using the name of a fictitious employee.
  • The Maine Attorney General's Office said the false reports were removed and that it had no knowledge of recent legitimate data breach reports from either VRChat or Discord.
  • Before the shutdown, submitted breach notices were automatically published to the public database without independent verification by the Attorney General's Office.
  • Companies can still submit breach notifications through the reporting service, but members of the public must contact the Attorney General's Office directly to obtain copies of disclosures.

Bankruptcy admin approves settlement fund of $47 million for 23andMe data breach victims — May Read

A Missouri bankruptcy court administrator approved a $46.8 million settlement fund for victims of 23andMe’s 2023 data breach, with about $32.5 million going to affected customers. Individual payouts will range from about $50 to $10,000 depending on the severity of harm.

  • A Missouri bankruptcy court administrator approved a $46.8 million settlement fund for victims of a 2023 data breach at 23andMe.
  • About 7 million 23andMe customers had data stolen by hackers starting in April 2023, and many had their information posted on the dark web.
  • Victims will receive $32.5 million from the settlement fund, while more than $14 million will be used to pay Kroll, the settlement and claims administrator.
  • Nearly 256,000 claims have been resolved, with class members eligible for awards ranging from $50 for minor claims to up to $10,000 for the most serious claims.
  • 23andMe, now named Chrome Holding Co., filed for bankruptcy in March 2025 and liquidated most of its assets before founder Anne Wojcicki bought it back.

Early Warning Signs of Supply-Chain Attacks Live in the Dark Web — Optional

Supply-chain attacks often leave early clues on dark web forums through offers of developer access, leaked repositories, API keys, OAuth tokens, CI/CD secrets, and vendor data before they become public incidents. Defenders should monitor these signals because seemingly ordinary access sales can expose trusted software build, deployment, and integration paths.

  • Flare researchers found that underground forums and marketplaces can contain early warning signs of software supply-chain attacks before they are publicly reported as incidents.
  • Posts advertising GitHub access, private repositories, source code, API keys, OAuth tokens, cloud credentials, or CI/CD data may be supply-chain relevant depending on the trust relationships that access touches.
  • The article says source-code exposure can reveal credentials, build logic, deployment workflows, internal service names, API documentation, and integrations, making it more than an intellectual-property issue.
  • Publicly reported incidents involving Vercel, Sportradar, Mistral AI, Shai-Hulud, LiteLLM, and malicious VS Code extensions illustrate how trusted tools, vendor systems, package publishing, and developer environments can create supply-chain risk.
  • The article recommends that defenders monitor exposed developer credentials, GitHub and GitLab access, package registry tokens, leaked repositories, CI/CD secrets, cloud keys, OAuth grants, and claims involving important vendors or software providers.

Rethinking MDR as Attackers and Defenders Embrace AI — Optional

Traditional MDR services are increasingly inadequate against AI-accelerated threats because human triage leaves many low-severity alerts unreviewed, where real incidents can hide. The article argues for AI-driven SOC operations that investigate every alert with consistent forensic depth and escalate only the few cases needing human action.

  • The article argues that traditional MDR services cannot keep up with AI-assisted attackers because human analysts still triage alerts in queues and often prioritize only higher-severity alerts.
  • The article states that approximately 60% of alerts go unreviewed across the industry, and Intezer’s analysis of 25 million alerts found that real threats can originate in low-severity and informational alerts.
  • The article says MDR investigation quality can vary based on the analyst on duty, queue depth, staffing, and time of day, which can lead to shallow investigations and missed attacker activity.
  • The article describes most MDR services as a black box in which customers receive escalations and summaries but cannot fully inspect investigation logic, evidence trails, or analyst reasoning.
  • The article claims an AI SOC can automatically investigate 100% of endpoint, identity, cloud, network, phishing, and SIEM alerts with forensic depth, sub-minute median triage time, and less than 2% requiring human escalation.

The JNUC 2026 session catalog is live — and the clock is ticking — Optional

Jamf has opened the JNUC 2026 session catalog, with programming focused on Apple platform management and enterprise AI. Attendees are encouraged to browse sessions and register before Just in Time pricing ends on July 1 for the September 23–25 event in Kansas City.

  • The JNUC 2026 session catalog is now live for attendees to browse and use to plan their agendas.
  • The theme of JNUC 2026 is the Apple platform for enterprise AI.
  • The program includes topics such as Mac workflows, mobility workflows, Mac and mobile compliance, reporting and risk reduction, integrations and APIs, end-user experience, and community, career and culture.
  • Just in Time pricing for JNUC 2026 is available only through July 1 before standard rates begin.
  • JNUC 2026 is scheduled for September 23–25, 2026, in Kansas City.

AppOmni Wins 2026 Intellyx Digital Innovator Award for Second Consecutive Year — Optional

AppOmni won the 2026 Intellyx Digital Innovator Award for the second year in a row, highlighting its positioning in SaaS and AI security. The company emphasized its AI-focused security capabilities, including visibility, governance, threat detection, and remediation for AI-powered SaaS environments.

  • AppOmni received the 2026 Intellyx Digital Innovator Award, marking its second consecutive year and third overall time earning the recognition.
  • AppOmni CEO Neill Occhiogrosso said the award validates the company’s innovation and leadership in SaaS and AI security as AI becomes increasingly integrated into SaaS platforms.
  • The article states that AI expands the SaaS attack surface because AI-enabled services interact with customer data, inherit user permissions, and can operate autonomously without security posture guardrails.
  • The AppOmni platform provides 24/7 visibility and guided response for securing AI in SaaS environments, including Marlin AI, AskOmni, and AgentGuard capabilities.
  • AppOmni says it protects more than 101 million SaaS user accounts, secures over 260 million exposed data records, and analyzes 2 billion security events per day.

item.title: CISA BOD 26-04, The Gentlemen RaaS, and Breach Reporting Integrity Issues
item.description: This coverage window was dominated by vulnerability management policy changes, ransomware infrastructure reporting, and data-handling failures affecting public trust in breach disclosures. The most consequential development for defenders is CISA’s BOD 26-04, which formalizes risk-based remediation...
item.content:encoded: This coverage window was dominated by vulnerability management policy changes, ransomware infrastructure reporting, and data-handling failures affecting public trust in breach disclosures. The most consequential development for defenders is CISA’s BOD 26-04, which formalizes risk-based remediation expectations for U.S. federal civilian agencies amid concern over slow patching and faster vulnerability weaponization. Separately, reporting on The Gentlemen ransomware ecosystem and abuse of public breach portals highlights how exposed infrastructure and weak verification processes continue to shape both operational risk and public incident narratives. CISA issued Binding Operational Directive 26-04 on June 10, 2026, replacing BOD 22-01 and BOD 19-02 with a risk-based vulnerability remediation framework for Federal Civilian Executive Branch agencies. The directive evaluates vulnerabilities using four binary criteria: whether the affected asset is publicly exposed, whether the CVE appears in CISA’s Known Exploited Vulnerabilities catalog, whether exploitation is automatable by an adversary, and whether exploitation grants total or partial control of the system. The resulting 16 risk combinations map to remediation timelines ranging from three days with mandatory forensic triage for the highest-risk vulnerabilities to deferral until the next system upgrade when none of the risk criteria apply. CISA cited declining remediation effectiveness and AI-accelerated vulnerability discovery and weaponization as drivers for the shift, noting that the 2026 Verizon DBIR found only 26% of KEV-listed vulnerabilities were fully remediated by organizations in 2025. Agencies are required to update vulnerability management policies immediately, revise remediation processes within 60 days, and meet the full Table 1 remediation timelines within 180 days. This policy context overlaps with Silent Push reporting on The Gentlemen ransomware-as-a-service operation, where affiliates reportedly favor internet-facing VPN and firewall appliances for initial access. Silent Push described earlier infrastructure research around a malware loader it named CountLoader, seen in .NET, PowerShell, and JScript variants during July and August 2025, and linked activity to Cobalt Strike watermarks associated with affiliate activity spanning Black Basta, LockBit, Qilin, and The Gentlemen. Silent Push’s article contextualized recent reporting by Brian Krebs that identified the person allegedly behind The Gentlemen ransomware operation, while emphasizing Silent Push’s prior focus on infrastructure and affiliate behavior. The company reported that two Cobalt Strike watermarks, 1473793097 and 1357776117, were tied to a single ransomware affiliate connected to multiple ransomware ecosystems, including Black Basta, LockBit, Qilin, and The Gentlemen. One notable infrastructure overlap involved IP address 91.107.247[.]163, which Silent Push says it flagged as Cobalt Strike command-and-control on February 8, 2026. Check Point later identified the same IP in an April 20, 2026 DFIR report on a The Gentlemen intrusion, reinforcing the reported linkage between previously observed infrastructure and later incident-response findings. The reporting portrays The Gentlemen not as an isolated ransomware brand, but as part of a broader affiliate-driven ecosystem with reusable tooling and cross-family operational ties. Kyushu Electric Power Co., Inc. disclosed that a missing external storage device may affect private data tied to as many as 10.9 million customer accounts. The drive was reportedly used on April 27 for a backup because of server storage capacity constraints and was later found missing from a server room cabinet on May 26. Data on the device included customer names, service location addresses, electricity usage data, telephone numbers, retail electricity provider names, and related information, but the company said it did not include bank account or credit card data. The company reported the incident to Japan’s Personal Information Protection Commission and relevant authorities, and media reports said a police report was filed on June 4. Kyushu Electric Power also said impacted customers would be notified individually. The incident is significant because of the scale of potentially affected customer records and the involvement of utility-sector customer data, even without direct payment-card or bank-account exposure. Separately, Maine’s public breach notification portal was abused to publish fraudulent data breach disclosures before their legitimacy could be verified. VRChat told BleepingComputer that a breach notification posted in the Maine Attorney General’s database was fake, submitted under a fictitious employee name, and did not indicate compromised systems or data. A suspicious filing allegedly from Discord claimed 10 million affected individuals but included unreliable indicators such as a Gmail contact, placeholder phone number, and inconsistent dates. The Maine Office of the Attorney General confirmed that anyone can submit a breach notification form and have it added to the portal without verification. The reporting highlights how public breach repositories can become vectors for misinformation when submission and publication processes lack validation, potentially causing reputational harm and public confusion around real versus fabricated incidents.
